perturbation.explanation: This change removes critical warranty assurances, specifically regarding defects in construction, manufacture, and material and compliance with applicable laws and regulations which creates a legal risk because medical devices have specific legal requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the warranty that the manufacturing and shipment of the product comply with applicable laws, regulations, and certification requirements, the contract contradicts 21 CFR Part 820, which mandates that medical device manufacturers establish and maintain a quality system that ensures devices meet specified requirements. Removing the warranty undermines the requirement for compliance with these quality system requirements.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the warranty that the manufacturing and shipment of the product comply with applicable laws, regulations, and certification requirements. This omission directly contradicts 21 CFR Part 820, which, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that medical device manufacturers establish and maintain a quality system to ensure devices meet specified requirements. By removing the warranty, the contract undermines the requirement for compliance, creating a significant legal risk, especially considering the original contract specifically included compliance with health and safety standards.

perturbation.original_text: Customer hereby covenants and agrees that it shall bear all costs and expenses related to the implementation of any such recall. In case of a recall of the Product, Philips shall at its sole cost provide full cooperation to Customer in order to achieve an efficient and effective recall by Customer. Philips shall provide such cooperation at its own costs, including internal organizational costs but not including the costs of repair, replacement, installation of Products and logistics related to the recall [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive]. For greater certainty, the obligations of the Parties pursuant to this clause 7.6 shall only apply in respect of Products sold or distributed on or after the Effective Date, and for clarity, to thwe extent there is a recall that involves any Product sold or distributed prior to the Effective Date, the covenants and obligations of the Parties pursuant to this clause 7.6 shall not apply (and shall be addressed by the terms of the Purchase Agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: Customer hereby covenants and agrees that it shall bear all costs and expenses related to the implementation of any such recall. In case of a recall of the Product, Philips shall provide cooperation to Customer to achieve an efficient and effective recall by Customer. For greater certainty, the obligations of the Parties pursuant to this clause 7.6 shall only apply in respect of Products sold or distributed on or after the Effective Date, and for clarity, to thwe extent there is a recall that involves any Product sold or distributed prior to the Effective Date, the covenants and obligations of the Parties pursuant to this clause 7.6 shall not apply (and shall be addressed by the terms of the Purchase Agreement).
perturbation.explanation: This alteration removes Philips' obligation to provide full cooperation at its own cost during a product recall. This directly contradicts requirements under many regulatory regimes where manufacturers have a responsibility to support recall efforts.  It introduces legal risk related to regulatory compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement for Philips to fully cooperate at its own cost, especially including internal organizational costs, during a product recall, the contract contradicts 21 CFR § 7.46. This regulation outlines the factors that the FDA will consider when assessing a recall strategy, including the firm's ability to effectively execute the recall. Philips' lack of mandatory, fully funded cooperation hinders the effective execution of the recall.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes Philips' obligation to provide cooperation at its own cost during a product recall. The Law Explanation points to 21 CFR § 7.46 as outlining factors for the FDA when assessing a recall strategy, including the firm's ability to effectively execute the recall. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes 21 CFR Part 7 which pertains to enforcement policy and recalls. Removing Philips' mandatory, fully funded cooperation could hinder the effective execution of the recall, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Philips shall maintain, and provide Customer reasonable access to, all records, both during and after the termination or expiration of this Agreement, in accordance with the Quality Agreement. The cost of any off-site storage of such records after the Term of this Agreement shall be borne by Customer and invoiced on a calendar quarter basis. After the Term, Customer may elect to have such records delivered to it upon reimbursing Philips for its reasonable costs of such transfer.
perturbation.changed_text: Philips shall maintain, and provide Customer reasonable access to, all records during the term of this agreement. The cost of any off-site storage of such records shall be borne by Philips.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates Philips' obligation to maintain records after the termination of the agreement and restricts Customer's access to records after termination. This is problematic for regulatory compliance as medical device manufacturers are typically required to maintain records for a specified period beyond the manufacturing or supply agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the record maintenance and access to only during the term of the agreement, the contract contradicts 21 CFR § 820.180, which requires manufacturers to maintain records for a defined period to demonstrate that the quality system is effective and that devices meet their specifications. Eliminating this post-termination obligation prevents Customer, the legal manufacturer, from fulfilling its regulatory responsibilities related to those records after the contract ends.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts 21 CFR § 820.180 because it eliminates Philips' obligation to maintain records after the termination of the agreement. This contradicts the regulatory requirement for medical device manufacturers to maintain records for a specified period beyond the manufacturing or supply agreement. The original contract ensured record maintenance beyond the agreement term, whereas the change limits it to only during the agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains '21 CFR Part 820 Quality System Regulation 820.1 – 820.250 Subpart M Records 820.180 – 820.198 § 820.180 General requirements,' directly relates to record-keeping requirements for medical devices, highlighting the importance of maintaining records as mandated by law. By restricting the record maintenance period, the modified text weakens this legal requirement.
